content:
# Exodus Chapter 20 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~44v–45r (PDF pp. 85–86); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 61–62 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_020_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_020_page_002.jpg`

---

### God Speaks — The Ten Words (vv. 1–2)

**1** Then God spoke all these words: **2** "I am YHWH your God, the one who brought you out of the land of Egypt, out of the place where you were enslaved.

---

### No Other Gods — No Idols (vv. 3–6)

**3** "You must have no other gods besides me.

**4** "You must not make for yourself an idol in any form — whether of anything in the heavens above, or on the earth below, or in the waters beneath the earth. **5** You must not bow down to them or serve them, because I, YHWH your God, am a jealous God. When parents reject me, I hold their children accountable — to the third and fourth generation of those who hate me — **6** but I show faithful love to a thousand generations of those who love me and keep my commands.

---

### The Name Must Not Be Carried Falsely (v. 7)

**7** "You must not carry the name of YHWH your God for a worthless purpose, because YHWH will not leave unpunished anyone who carries his name for what is empty.

---

### Remember the Sabbath (vv. 8–11)

**8** "Remember the Sabbath day and keep it holy. **9** Six days you are to labor and do all your work, **10** but the seventh day is a Sabbath belonging to YHWH your God. On it you must not do any work — not you, nor your son or daughter, nor your male or female servant, nor your livestock, nor any foreigner living within your gates. **11** Because in six days YHWH made the heavens and the earth, the sea, and everything in them, and he rested on the seventh day. That is why YHWH blessed the Sabbath day and made it holy.

---

### Honor Your Parents (v. 12)

**12** "Honor your father and your mother, so that you may live long in the land that YHWH your God is giving you.

---

### The Five Prohibitions (vv. 13–17)

**13** "You must not murder.

**14** "You must not commit adultery.

**15** "You must not steal.

**16** "You must not testify falsely against your neighbor.

**17** "You must not covet your neighbor's house. You must not covet your neighbor's wife, or his male or female servant, or his ox or donkey, or anything that belongs to your neighbor."

---

### The People's Terror — Give Us a Mediator (vv. 18–21)

**18** When the people experienced the thunder and the lightning flashes, the sound of the trumpet and the mountain smoking, they trembled and stood at a distance. **19** They said to Moses, "You speak to us, and we will listen — but do not let God speak to us, or we will die."

**20** Moses said to the people, "Do not be afraid. God has come to test you, so that the fear of him will be always before you and keep you from sinning." **21** The people stayed at a distance while Moses approached the thick darkness where God was.

---

### The Altar Law — How to Worship (vv. 22–26)

**22** YHWH said to Moses, "This is what you are to say to the Israelites: 'You yourselves have seen that I spoke to you from heaven. **23** You must not make gods of silver alongside me, and you must not make for yourselves gods of gold.

**24** "'Build an altar of earth for me, and sacrifice on it your burnt offerings and your peace offerings, your sheep and your cattle. In every place where I cause my name to be remembered, I will come to you and bless you. **25** If you build me an altar of stone, you must not construct it with cut stones, because if you use a tool on it, you will have profaned it. **26** And you must not go up to my altar on steps, so that your nakedness is not exposed on it.'"

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**v.1 — "GOD SPOKE ALL THESE WORDS" (Heb. *vayedabber Elohim et kol-haddevarim ha'elleh lemor*, וַיְדַבֵּ֣ר אֱלֹהִ֔ים אֵ֛ת כׇּל־הַדְּבָרִ֥ים הָאֵ֖לֶּה לֵאמֹֽר):** The subject is *Elohim* (אלהים, "God") rather than *YHWH* — the text uses the more universal name at the moment of the most particular revelation. The term *devarim* (דברים, "words") is the same word that gives the book of Deuteronomy its Hebrew name. These are not "commandments" in the Hebrew — they are *devarim*, "words" or "utterances." Jewish tradition calls them *Aseret Hadibberot* (עשרת הדברות, "the Ten Words" or "Ten Utterances"), not "the Ten Commandments." The distinction matters: these are divine speech-acts, not merely regulations. They are the direct, unmediated voice of God speaking to an assembled nation. The LXX renders it *elalēsen Kyrios pantas tous logous toutous* (ἐλάλησεν κύριος πάντας τοὺς λόγους τούτους), using *logous* (λόγους, "words") — the plural of *logos*. Each commandment is a *logos*, a word that structures reality.

The word *lemor* (לאמר, "saying") introduces direct speech. What follows is not Moses' paraphrase or summary — it is God's own voice. Deuteronomy 5:4 makes this explicit: "Face to face YHWH spoke with you at the mountain, out of the fire." This is the one moment in all of Scripture where God speaks publicly, to an entire assembled people, without a mediator's filter. The rabbis debated whether the people heard all ten words directly or only the first two (the *anokhi* and the prohibition of other gods) before the experience became unbearable and they begged Moses to mediate the rest (see note on v.19).

**v.2 — THE *ANOKHI* SELF-IDENTIFICATION: "I AM YHWH YOUR GOD" (Heb. *anokhi YHWH Elohekha asher hotsetikha me'erets Mitsrayim mibbeyt avadim*, אָנֹכִ֖י יְהוָ֣ה אֱלֹהֶ֑יךָ אֲשֶׁ֧ר הוֹצֵאתִ֛יךָ מֵאֶ֥רֶץ מִצְרַ֖יִם מִבֵּ֥ית עֲבָדִֽים):** This verse is the theological foundation on which every commandment rests. Jewish tradition counts it as the first "word" (the first of the ten *dibberot*), while Christian traditions typically treat it as a preamble to the first commandment. The difference is significant: if the self-identification is itself a commandment, then the first obligation is not to *do* anything but to *know who is speaking*.

The pronoun *anokhi* (אנכי, "I") is the emphatic first-person singular — longer and more solemn than the common *ani* (אני). It is the royal, covenantal "I." The self-identification follows the pattern of ancient Near Eastern suzerainty treaties, where the great king identifies himself before listing the vassal's obligations: "I am [name], the great king, who did [great deeds] for you; therefore you shall [obligations]." The Decalogue follows this structure exactly. But there is a crucial difference: in suzerainty treaties, the king identifies himself by his titles, his conquests, his territory. YHWH identifies himself by a *relationship* and an *act of liberation*. "I am YHWH your God" — *Elohekha* (אלהיך, "your God"), second person singular, intimate, personal. Not "the god of the cosmos" or "the supreme deity" but *your* God. And the identifying deed is not conquest but rescue: *asher hotsetikha me'erets Mitsrayim* (אשר הוצאתיך מארץ מצרים, "who brought you out of the land of Egypt").

The phrase *mibbeyt avadim* (מבית עבדים, "from the house of slaves") is devastating in its compression. Egypt is not named as a country or a civilization; it is named as a *house of slavery*. The word *bayit* (בית, "house") is domestic — Egypt was not a foreign land but a prison-household. The word *avadim* (עבדים, "slaves, servants") is the plural of *eved* (עבד), the same root that means "to serve, to worship." Israel was taken from a house of *service-to-Pharaoh* and brought to a mountain of *service-to-YHWH*. The entire Decalogue is framed by this exchange of masters: you were enslaved to Egypt; now you belong to me. Every commandment that follows is addressed to a liberated people — obedience is the practice of freedom, not the imposition of a new bondage.

The LXX renders this *ego eimi Kyrios ho Theos sou, hostis exēgagon se ek gēs Aigyptou, ex oikou douleias* (ἐγώ εἰμι κύριος ὁ θεός σου, ὅστις ἐξήγαγόν σε ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου, ἐξ οἴκου δουλείας). The phrase *ego eimi* (ἐγώ εἰμι, "I am") is the same construction that appears on the lips of Jesus in John's Gospel (John 8:58, "Before Abraham was, *ego eimi*") — the Johannine echo is unmistakable.

**v.3 — "NO OTHER GODS BESIDES ME" (Heb. *lo yihyeh-lekha elohim acherim al-panai*, לֹ֣א יִהְיֶ֣ה־לְךָ֩ אֱלֹהִ֨ים אֲחֵרִ֜ים עַל־פָּנָֽיַ):** The phrase *al-panai* (על פני) is notoriously difficult. It can mean "before me" (in my presence), "besides me" (in addition to me), "in my face" (as an affront to me), or "over against me" (in opposition to me). The ambiguity is probably intentional: every possible meaning applies. No other gods in my presence, beside me, in my face, or against me. The prohibition is total.

The text does not deny that other gods *exist* — it prohibits Israel from *having* them. The verb *yihyeh* (יהיה, "there shall be") with *lekha* (לך, "for you, belonging to you") is about possession and allegiance: "there shall not *belong to you* other gods." Whether other gods are real spiritual entities, demonic powers, or human fabrications is not addressed here. The commandment operates at the level of loyalty: whatever they are, they are not yours. You belong to YHWH; YHWH belongs to you. The covenant is exclusive.

**vv.4–6 — THE IDOL PROHIBITION AND THE JEALOUS GOD:** The second word (in Protestant reckoning) or the continuation of the first (in Jewish and Catholic reckoning) prohibits *pesel* (פסל, "carved image, idol") and *temunah* (תמונה, "form, likeness, representation") of anything in the three-tiered cosmos: *bashamayim mima'al* (בשמים ממעל, "in the heavens above"), *ba'arets mitachat* (בארץ מתחת, "on the earth below"), *bammayim mitachat la'arets* (במים מתחת לארץ, "in the waters under the earth"). The prohibition is comprehensive: no image of any celestial, terrestrial, or aquatic creature may serve as a representation of the divine. The three-tiered cosmology (heaven-earth-water) corresponds to the three domains of Genesis 1: the sky, the land, and the sea. Every creature in every domain is excluded as a candidate for divine representation.

The word *qanna* (קנא, "jealous, zealous") in v.5 describes YHWH as *El qanna* (אל קנא, "a jealous God"). The term *qin'ah* (קנאה) denotes the fierce, protective passion of a spouse whose partner has been unfaithful. It is not petty jealousy but covenantal fury — the righteous rage of betrayed love. The marriage metaphor is implicit here and becomes explicit in Hosea, Jeremiah, and Ezekiel: YHWH is the husband; Israel is the wife; idolatry is adultery. The "jealousy" of God is the intensity of divine commitment refusing to share what is covenantally exclusive.

The phrase *poqed avon avot al-banim al-shilleshim ve'al-ribbe'im lesone'ai* (פֹּקֵ֣ד עֲוֺ֣ן אָבֹ֡ת עַל־בָּנִים֩ עַל־שִׁלֵּשִׁ֨ים וְעַל־רִבֵּעִ֜ים לְשֹׂנְאָ֗י, "visiting the iniquity of fathers on children, to the third and fourth generation of those who hate me") has been the most theologically contested phrase in the Decalogue. The verb *poqed* (פקד, "visiting, attending to, calling to account") does not mean simple punishment-transfer; it means that the *consequences* of parental sin ripple through subsequent generations. A father's idolatry shapes his household's culture; children inherit not the guilt but the distorted world their parents created. Ezekiel 18 will later clarify that each person bears their own guilt — the point here is about the *social reach* of sin, not juridical transfer of guilt. The limitation to "third and fourth" generation (roughly the span of a living family unit) contrasts sharply with the *thousand generations* of v.6 — the ratio of judgment to mercy is roughly 1:250. Mercy vastly outweighs judgment.

I chose "when parents reject me, I hold their children accountable" to capture the dynamic force of *poqed* — not mechanistic punishment-transfer, but the relational consequences of parental betrayal reverberating through family systems.

**v.7 — "YOU MUST NOT CARRY THE NAME" (Heb. *lo tissa et-shem-YHWH Elohekha lashshav ki lo yenaqqeh YHWH et asher-yissa et-shemo lashshav*, לֹ֥א תִשָּׂ֛א אֶת־שֵֽׁם־יְהוָ֥ה אֱלֹהֶ֖יךָ לַשָּׁ֑וְא כִּ֣י לֹ֤א יְנַקֶּה֙ יְהוָ֔ה אֵ֛ת אֲשֶׁר־יִשָּׂ֥א אֶת־שְׁמ֖וֹ לַשָּֽׁוְא):** This commandment is almost universally mistranslated as "You shall not take the name of the Lord your God in vain." The Hebrew verb is *tissa* (תשא, from *nasa*, נשא, "to lift, to carry, to bear"), not *laqach* (לקח, "to take"). The difference is enormous. *Nasa* means to carry something on your person, to bear it as an identity marker, to be weighted by it. In ancient Near Eastern covenant practice, a vassal "bore" or "carried" the name of his suzerain — the name was a mark of belonging, allegiance, and representation. To *carry* YHWH's name *lashshav* (לשוא, "for emptiness, for falsehood, for nothing") is to claim to represent YHWH while living in a way that empties that representation of meaning.

The word *shav* (שוא, "emptiness, falsehood, worthlessness, vanity") encompasses far more than profanity or casual swearing. It includes: (1) false oaths — invoking YHWH's name to guarantee a lie; (2) empty worship — going through the motions of YHWH-devotion without substance; (3) misrepresentation — claiming divine authorization for human agendas; (4) trivialization — treating the sacred name as a casual expletive. The traditional focus on "swearing" captures only the first of these four dimensions. The commandment is about *bearing* a name — carrying the divine identity — and the prohibition is against doing so in a way that empties, falsifies, or trivializes it.

I translated *tissa* as "carry" rather than "take" to preserve this crucial nuance. The phrase *lo yenaqqeh* (לא ינקה, "he will not leave unpunished / he will not acquit") uses the Piel of *naqah* (נקה, "to be clean, to be innocent"). YHWH will not *clean* the record of someone who falsifies his name. This is the only commandment that includes its own explicit penalty clause within the commandment itself.

**vv.8–11 — THE SABBATH: THE LONGEST COMMANDMENT:** The Sabbath commandment is the most detailed of the ten, receiving more text than any other — an indication of its centrality.

The verb *zakhor* (זכור, "remember") in v.8 is an infinitive absolute used as an imperative — it has a force closer to "keep remembering" or "never forget." The parallel in Deuteronomy 5:12 uses *shamor* (שמור, "observe, guard, keep") instead of *zakhor*. The Talmud (Shevuot 20b) and the Friday-evening hymn *Lekha Dodi* resolve the discrepancy by declaring that God spoke both words simultaneously — *zakhor veshamor bedibbur echad* (זכור ושמור בדבור אחד, "remember and observe in a single utterance") — something impossible for a human mouth but possible for God. The two verbs define complementary dimensions: *zakhor* is the inward, cognitive dimension (hold the meaning of Sabbath in your consciousness); *shamor* is the outward, behavioral dimension (guard the day through specific practices of rest and abstention).

The word *Shabbat* (שבת) derives from the root *shavat* (שבת, "to cease, to stop, to rest"). It does not primarily mean "relaxation" or "recreation" but *cessation* — the deliberate stopping of productive activity. The theological rationale given here (v.11) is creation: *ki sheshet-yamim asah YHWH et-hashamayim ve'et-ha'arets* (כי ששת ימים עשה יהוה את השמים ואת הארץ, "for in six days YHWH made the heavens and the earth"). By resting on the seventh day, Israel imitates the Creator — the Sabbath is an act of *imitatio Dei*. The Deuteronomy version gives a different rationale: liberation from Egypt (Deut. 5:15). The two rationales are complementary: Sabbath celebrates both cosmic order (creation) and social justice (liberation). A slave cannot rest; only a free person can choose to stop working. The Sabbath is an embodied declaration of freedom.

The scope of Sabbath rest is remarkably inclusive (v.10): *attah uvinekha-uvittekha avdekha va'amatekha uvehemtekha vegerekha asher bish'arekha* (אתה ובנך ובתך עבדך ואמתך ובהמתך וגרך אשר בשעריך, "you and your son and your daughter, your male servant and your female servant, and your livestock, and the foreigner within your gates"). The rest extends to children, servants, animals, and resident aliens — every being within the Israelite household. The servant rests not because the servant chose to but because the master is *commanded* to stop requiring labor. The Sabbath is the first labor law in history: a divinely mandated day off for everyone, including those with no power to demand it for themselves.

**v.12 — HONOR YOUR FATHER AND MOTHER (Heb. *kabbed et-avikha ve'et-immekha lema'an ya'arikhun yamekha al ha'adamah asher-YHWH Elohekha noten lakh*, כַּבֵּ֥ד אֶת־אָבִ֖יךָ וְאֶת־אִמֶּ֑ךָ לְמַ֨עַן֙ יַאֲרִכ֣וּן יָמֶ֔יךָ עַ֚ל הָ֣אֲדָמָ֔ה אֲשֶׁר־יְהוָ֥ה אֱלֹהֶ֖יךָ נֹתֵ֥ן לָֽךְ):** The verb *kabbed* (כבד, Piel imperative of *kavad*, כבד, "to be heavy, to be weighty") means to treat as weighty, substantial, significant. The root *k-b-d* is the same root that gives us *kavod* (כבוד, "glory, honor, weight") — the word used for God's own glory. To "honor" parents is to give them *kavod*, to treat them with the same gravity with which one treats the divine. This is the "bridge commandment" — positioned between the vertical obligations (toward God, vv.2-11) and the horizontal obligations (toward neighbor, vv.13-17). Parents occupy a mediating position: they are human, but they function as representatives of divine authority in the household. Honoring parents bridges the God-ward and neighbor-ward dimensions of covenantal life.

This is the only commandment with a promise attached: *lema'an ya'arikhun yamekha* (למען יארכון ימיך, "so that your days may be prolonged"). The promise is collective as much as individual — a society that honors its elders endures; one that discards them collapses. Paul quotes this as the "first commandment with a promise" (Eph. 6:2).

**v.13 — "YOU MUST NOT MURDER" (Heb. *lo tirtsach*, לֹ֥֖א תִּֿרְצָֽ֖ח):** The verb *ratsach* (רצח) is specific: it denotes unlawful, premeditated, or unauthorized killing. It is distinct from *harag* (הרג, "to kill" — general term), *hemit* (המית, "to put to death" — judicial execution), and *muth* (מות, "to die"). The choice of *ratsach* rather than the more general *harag* indicates that the prohibition is against *murder* — the unjust taking of human life — not against all forms of killing (which would contradict the Torah's own provisions for capital punishment, warfare, and animal slaughter). I translated "murder" rather than "kill" to preserve this distinction.

**v.14 — "YOU MUST NOT COMMIT ADULTERY" (Heb. *lo tin'af*, לֹ֣֖א תִּֿנְאָֽ֑ף):** The verb *na'af* (נאף) specifically denotes sexual intercourse with another man's wife — it is a violation of the marriage covenant. In the ancient Israelite context, adultery was primarily understood as a property and covenant violation: it violated the exclusive bond between husband and wife and disrupted the social fabric of kinship. The commandment protects the covenant of marriage as a reflection of YHWH's own covenantal exclusivity (see note on *qanna*, v.5). The prophets (Hosea, Jeremiah, Ezekiel) will use *na'af* metaphorically for Israel's idolatry — "adultery" against YHWH. The sexual and the theological are intertwined.

**v.15 — "YOU MUST NOT STEAL" (Heb. *lo tignov*, לֹ֣֖א תִּֿגְנֹֽ֔ב):** The verb *ganav* (גנב) means to steal by stealth (as opposed to *gazal*, גזל, "to rob by force"). Rabbinic interpretation (Mekhilta, Sanhedrin 86a) argued that since the surrounding commandments (murder, adultery) carry the death penalty, *lo tignov* in this context refers to *kidnapping* — the stealing of a human person — rather than theft of property (which is addressed in Exod. 22:1-4). Whether or not one accepts this narrowing, the placement of *lo tignov* among capital offenses elevates it beyond petty larceny.

**v.16 — "YOU MUST NOT TESTIFY FALSELY" (Heb. *lo ta'aneh vere'akha ed shaqer*, לֹֽא־תַעֲנֶ֥ה בְרֵעֲךָ֖ עֵ֥ד שָֽׁקֶר):** The verb *ta'aneh* (תענה, from *anah*, ענה, "to answer, to testify, to respond") is specifically legal: it refers to giving testimony in a judicial proceeding. The word *shaqer* (שקר, "falsehood, lie, deception") denotes deliberate untruth. The Deuteronomy parallel uses *shav* (שוא, "empty, vain") instead of *shaqer* — the same word from the third commandment. The commandment protects the judicial system: without truthful testimony, justice collapses. In a society without forensic technology, eyewitness testimony was the primary basis for legal judgment. False witness could result in an innocent person's execution (cf. the Naboth narrative, 1 Kings 21). I translated "testify falsely" rather than "bear false witness" to make the legal context explicit.

**v.17 — "YOU MUST NOT COVET" (Heb. *lo tachmod beyt re'ekha lo tachmod eshet re'ekha ve'avdo va'amato veshoro vachamoro vekhol asher lere'ekha*, לֹ֥א תַחְמֹ֖ד בֵּ֣ית רֵעֶ֑ךָ לֹֽא־תַחְמֹ֞ד אֵ֣שֶׁת רֵעֶ֗ךָ וְעַבְדּ֤וֹ וַאֲמָתוֹ֙ וְשׁוֹר֣וֹ וַחֲמֹר֔וֹ וְכֹ֖ל אֲשֶׁ֥ר לְרֵעֶֽךָ):** The verb *chamad* (חמד, "to desire, to covet, to take delight in") is not merely an emotion — in Hebrew usage it implies desire that leads to action, the craving that produces scheming. Compare 2 Samuel 11, where David *sees* Bathsheba, *desires* her, and *takes* her — the sequence of sight-desire-seizure is precisely what *chamad* encompasses. The Deuteronomy parallel adds a second verb, *tit'avveh* (תתאוה, from *avah*, אוה, "to crave, to long for"), distinguishing desire (*tit'avveh*) from acquisitive scheming (*tachmod*). This is the only commandment that legislates the interior life — not just action, but the desire that precedes action. It is the most "impossible" commandment, because desire cannot simply be switched off by willpower. It stands as a diagnostic: if the other nine commandments address what you *do*, the tenth addresses what you *are*. The Decalogue begins with the identity of God (v.2) and ends with the desires of the human heart (v.17) — from the divine *anokhi* ("I am") to the human *tachmod* ("you want").

The list of coveted items — house, wife, servant, ox, donkey, and "anything that belongs to your neighbor" — is arranged from immovable property (*bayit*, house) to movable property to the comprehensive catch-all *vekhol asher lere'ekha* (וכל אשר לרעך, "everything that is your neighbor's"). The final phrase transforms the commandment from a specific list to a universal principle: do not orient your desire toward what belongs to another.

**vv.18–19 — THE PEOPLE'S TERROR: UNMEDIATED GOD IS UNBEARABLE (Heb. v.18: *vekhol-ha'am ro'im et-haqqolot ve'et-hallappidim ve'et qol hashshofar ve'et-hahar ashen vayyar ha'am vayyanu'u vayya'amdu merachoq*, וְכׇל־הָעָם֩ רֹאִ֨ים אֶת־הַקּוֹלֹ֜ת וְאֶת־הַלַּפִּידִ֗ם וְאֵת֙ ק֣וֹל הַשֹּׁפָ֔ר וְאֶת־הָהָ֖ר עָשֵׁ֑ן וַיַּ֤רְא הָעָם֙ וַיָּנֻ֔עוּ וַיַּֽעַמְד֖וּ מֵרָחֹֽק):** The most remarkable feature of v.18 is the synesthesia: *ro'im et-haqqolot* (ראים את הקולות, "seeing the sounds/voices"). The people *see* what should be *heard*. The thunders/voices become visible. This is either a literary description of overwhelming sensory confusion — the senses collapse into one another under theophanic overload — or a deliberate statement that at Sinai the normal boundaries between perception broke down. The Mekhilta records: "They saw what is normally heard and heard what is normally seen." The LXX normalizes this, rendering it as "the people perceived" (*heōra*, ἑώρα), but the Hebrew preserves the strangeness. God's speech does not arrive through a single sensory channel; it overwhelms all channels simultaneously.

The verb *vayyanu'u* (וינעו, from *nu'a*, נוע, "to shake, to sway, to stagger") describes the people swaying or staggering — physically destabilized by what they perceive. They then *stand at a distance* (*vayya'amdu merachoq*, ויעמדו מרחק). The theological movement is retreat: the people, who in 19:17 were brought *to the foot of the mountain*, now pull back. They have experienced unmediated divine speech and cannot bear it.

**v.19** records their plea: *dabber-attah immanu venishma'ah ve'al-yedabber immanu Elohim pen-namut* (דַּבֵּר־אַתָּ֤ה עִמָּ֙נוּ֙ וְנִשְׁמָ֔עָה וְאַל־יְדַבֵּ֥ר עִמָּ֛נוּ אֱלֹהִ֖ים פֶּן־נָמֽוּת, "You speak with us and we will hear, but do not let God speak with us, lest we die"). This is the birth of prophetic mediation as a permanent institution. The people voluntarily request a buffer between themselves and God. They prefer Moses' voice to God's voice — not because they distrust God, but because the direct encounter is lethal to their humanity. The verb *pen-namut* (פן נמות, "lest we die") is not hypothetical politeness — they genuinely believe they will die. Deuteronomy 5:25 expands: "For who of all flesh has heard the voice of the living God speaking from the fire, as we have, and lived?" The question is existential: unmediated divine encounter annihilates the finite creature.

**v.20 — FEAR AS PEDAGOGY (Heb. *vayyomer Mosheh el-ha'am al-tira'u ki le'ba'avur nassot etkhem ba ha'Elohim uva'avur tihyeh yir'ato al-peneikhem levilti techeta'u*, וַיֹּ֨אמֶר מֹשֶׁ֣ה אֶל־הָעָם֮ אַל־תִּירָאוּ֒ כִּ֗י לְבַעֲבוּר֙ נַסּ֣וֹת אֶתְכֶ֔ם בָּ֖א הָאֱלֹהִ֑ים וּבַעֲב֗וּר תִּהְיֶ֧ה יִרְאָת֛וֹ עַל־פְּנֵיכֶ֖ם לְבִלְתִּ֥י תֶחֱטָֽאוּ):** Moses' response contains a paradox: "Do not *fear* (*al-tira'u*, אל תיראו)... so that his *fear* (*yir'ato*, יראתו) will be on your faces." The same root (*yare'*, ירא, "to fear") is used in both the prohibition and the purpose. Do not fear *with terror* — but let the *awe of God* remain permanently on you. There are two kinds of fear: the panic that paralyzes (*tira'u*) and the reverence that restrains (*yir'ah*). The theophany is designed to replace the first with the second. The terror of Sinai is not an end in itself; it is a pedagogical device to implant permanent reverence. The phrase *al-peneikhem* (על פניכם, "on your faces") suggests a visible, worn quality — awe as something that shows on the countenance, that others can see.

The verb *nassot* (נסות, from *nasah*, נסה, "to test, to try, to prove") means God is *testing* Israel through this experience. The test is whether the overwhelming encounter will produce lasting reverence or only momentary terror. The goal — *levilti techeta'u* (לבלתי תחטאו, "so that you do not sin") — reveals the practical purpose of theophanic awe: it functions as a moral restraint. The memory of Sinai's fire and thunder is meant to accompany every future ethical decision.

**v.21 — MOSES ENTERS THE THICK DARKNESS (Heb. *vayya'amod ha'am merachoq uMosheh niggash el-ha'arafel asher-sham ha'Elohim*, וַיַּעֲמֹ֥ד הָעָ֖ם מֵרָחֹ֑ק וּמֹשֶׁה֙ נִגַּ֔שׁ אֶל־הָֽעֲרָפֶ֖ל אֲשֶׁר־שָׁ֥ם הָאֱלֹהִֽים):** The contrast is stark: the people stand *far off*; Moses draws *near*. The word *arafel* (ערפל, "thick darkness, deep gloom") is distinct from *anan* (ענן, "cloud") and *choshekh* (חשך, "darkness"). The *arafel* is the innermost layer of divine concealment — denser than cloud, darker than ordinary darkness. It is the place *where God is* (*asher-sham ha'Elohim*, אשר שם האלהים). God dwells in a darkness so thick it cannot be penetrated by human sight. This is the origin of the mystical tradition of *apophatic theology* — knowing God through what God is *not*, encountering the divine in the darkness beyond all images and concepts. Gregory of Nyssa's *Life of Moses* and the anonymous *Cloud of Unknowing* both draw on this verse: the highest knowledge of God is found not in light but in the *arafel*, the luminous darkness where God hides.

**vv.22–23 — "YOU HAVE SEEN THAT I SPOKE FROM HEAVEN" (Heb. *attem re'item ki min-hashamayim dibbarti immakhem*, אַתֶּ֣ם רְאִיתֶ֔ם כִּ֚י מִן־הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם דִּבַּ֖רְתִּי עִמָּכֶֽם):** There is a tension here: in ch. 19, YHWH *descended* on the mountain; here he says he spoke *from heaven*. Was God on the mountain or in heaven? The rabbis resolved this by saying God bent the heavens down to touch the mountain — heaven and earth met at Sinai. The prohibition against silver and gold gods (*elohei khesef ve'elohei zahav*, אלהי כסף ואלהי זהב, "gods of silver and gods of gold") immediately after the theophany links the idol prohibition to the experience of hearing God speak without seeing any form. Deuteronomy 4:12 makes this explicit: "You heard the sound of words but saw no form — only a voice." The absence of visual form at Sinai is the theological foundation of the image prohibition: you cannot make what you did not see.

**vv.24–26 — THE ALTAR LAW: EARTH, UNCUT STONE, NO STEPS:** The chapter closes with altar regulations that are strikingly primitive — deliberately so.

The *mizbach adamah* (מזבח אדמה, "altar of earth") is the simplest possible worship structure: a mound of dirt. This is worship stripped to its essence — no architecture, no craftsmanship, no display. The phrase *bekhol-hammaqom asher azkir et-shemi* (בכל המקום אשר אזכיר את שמי, "in every place where I cause my name to be remembered") is remarkable: it authorizes worship at multiple locations, not a single central shrine. This stands in tension with Deuteronomy 12's centralization of worship and suggests an earlier stratum of Israelite practice when YHWH could be worshipped anywhere God chose to manifest.

The prohibition of *hewn stone* (*lo-tivneh ethen gazit*, לא תבנה אתהן גזית, v.25) is explained: *ki charbkha henafta aleha vattechalelha* (כי חרבך הנפת עליה ותחללה, "for if you wield your tool on it, you profane it"). The word *cherev* (חרב) means both "tool/chisel" and "sword." Human technology profanes the altar. The natural, unworked stone retains its creational integrity; the chisel introduces human will and artifice into what should be purely God's domain. Joshua 8:31 records that Joshua built an altar of *uncut stones* in obedience to this command.

The prohibition of *steps* (v.26) — *velo ta'aleh vema'alot al-mizbechi asher lo-tiggaleh ervatekha alav* (ולא תעלה במעלות על מזבחי אשר לא תגלה ערותך עליו, "you shall not go up by steps to my altar, so that your nakedness not be exposed on it") — protects dignity in worship. The concern about *ervah* (ערוה, "nakedness, exposure") while ascending steps reflects the practical reality that ancient Near Eastern garments (long robes without undergarments) would expose the body when climbing. The later priestly garments include *mikhnesei-vad* (מכנסי בד, "linen undergarments," Exod. 28:42) precisely to address this concern. The altar law insists that worship not involve exposure or humiliation — God's presence demands both simplicity and dignity.

---

**The theology of Exodus 20 — the architecture of covenant life:**

This chapter contains the most famous text in the Hebrew Bible and arguably the most influential moral document in human history. Three theological structures define the Ten Words.

**1. Identity before ethics.** The Decalogue does not begin with a command but with a self-identification: "I am YHWH your God, who brought you out of Egypt." Before any obligation is stated, the Obligator is named and his saving act is recalled. Obedience is not the means of entering relationship with God — it is the *response* to a relationship already established. Israel does not earn its liberation by keeping commandments; Israel keeps commandments because it has already been liberated. The sequence is grace-identity-ethic, never ethic-identity-grace. This is the permanent structure of biblical morality: you are loved first, then called to live accordingly.

**2. The movement from God to neighbor, mediated by desire.** The ten words move from the vertical (God: vv.2-11) through the pivotal (parents: v.12) to the horizontal (neighbor: vv.13-17). The final commandment — against coveting — turns inward: from actions to desires, from the external to the internal. The Decalogue traces the full circle of human moral life: upward toward God, outward toward community, and inward toward the heart. The tenth commandment acknowledges that external compliance without internal transformation is insufficient. The law that began with God's identity ends by interrogating human desire.

**3. The unbearable directness of God.** The people's terrified retreat (vv.18-19) and their demand for mediation reveals a permanent truth about the human condition: we cannot sustain direct encounter with the holy. We need mediators, interpreters, buffers. Moses enters the *arafel* so Israel does not have to. The prophetic and priestly institutions of Israel, and ultimately the Christian doctrine of incarnation, grow from this Sinai moment: God's voice is too much for unshielded humanity, so God speaks through chosen vessels. The paradox is that what the people most need — direct knowledge of God — is exactly what they cannot survive. The mediator is not a second-best solution; the mediator is the form divine love takes when it encounters fragile creatures who would be destroyed by unfiltered glory.

The Ten Words are spoken once, directly, to an entire nation — and never again. From this point forward, God speaks through Moses. The directness of Sinai is a one-time event whose memory is meant to sustain all future mediated revelation. Israel will never again hear the voice themselves; they will remember having heard it, and that memory will give authority to every prophet who comes after, claiming to speak in the name of the God who once spoke for himself.

— *PNEUMA*
